column6: message
How about just not using words which offend people around people offended by those words?

Didn't realize you were gay! As they say, when you assume, etc. etc. 

I understand what you are saying- the idea that more people freely using these words in the reclaimed sense, despite their membership in minority groups, dilutes the power the word has as a slur. That's a good point, and one I'd forgotten about. And while I agree that if a gay friend of yours calls you "fag" with your previously expressed permission that's fine, I feel like if a straight friend does it there can be mixed messages- not between you and your friend, but between your friend and *other straight people*. 

Because despite "Faggot" being mostly reclaimed by the gay community (and as you know, there are debates within the community on whether it is appropriate for gay men to call themselves that), the optics of a straight person calling a gay man a faggot communicates to straight people as a whole that the word is acceptable for them to use- and regardless of if it's okay for gay men to use it, I think it's NOT okay for straight people to use it! 

I may not be explaining this very well, but I hope I'm getting my overall point across!

column6: Once we reach a certain technological point, we stop evolving because we can mold the world around us to ensure survival. Natural selection, in most of its aspects, has already stopped occurring for humanity. What makes you think we will keep evolving? We would have to regress technologically for that to happen.

Okay, to avoid unneccesary arguments, I'm not going to disagree with the biological prescriptivism you've adopted. You've made some fairly standard, and to my mind accurate, observations of our existence genetically, and then you've used that to make some "should" claims.

The first "should" claim is that we "should" work towards the perpetuation of humanity.

Okay, I'll roll with that.

Secondly, you've made the observation that humanity can't exist on Earth perpetually, for two reasons.

Firstly, "In a few billion years the Sun will engulf Earth".

Unfortunately, your static concept of "humanity" will explode way before the sun does. Evolution is constantly in flux. When you say "assuming we'll survive long enough on Earth for that to happen" simply makes no sense to me. There simply won't be any humans then, no matter what. If I follow biology and ecology as our moral rule book, I might as well say it is our duty as a species to rise and fall.

The second reason you give is that we're "destroying the Earth" and some of these factors "we can do nothing about".

What I find odd is that you're quite willing to talk of possibilities of FTL travel, and treat rules of physics with a degree of skepticism (and sure, why not, we don't know what we don't know), but then take a fatalist attitude to caretaking of the environment.

Presumably space-faring people will sustain themselves in one way or another, either on ships or on planets. People who subscribe to the spacefaring civilization model seem quite happy saying we'll do a reasonable job creating biospheres on yet-to-be-built ships, or terraform Mars, but will in the same breath say the primary motive for this is to escape a planet we're wrecking, despite being evolved to live here, and having intricate life support mechanisms which have evolved over billions of years.

I'm not going to make a claim as to which mindset is best to go with, but I don't really see how you can get away with using both when it best suits you.

column6:
Because race doesn't play on someones personal belief. That's the major difference. 

Everyone is entitled to their beliefs. So if a resturaunt owner wanted to refuse a cake because the marriage itself is not something they would support, that is their decision. It is a private business. Besides, and what I don't think gets talked enough about is, *why would you want that company to bake you a cake?* If that company doesn't support you, why would you support them by forcing them to bake a cake and pay for it? 

A gay couple will do more damage by spreading the word. While in their community saying "don't support this cake shop, support this one instead" and that's how you solve ignorance. People will still remain in their belief but that doesn't mean the owner won't consider the money they are losing. 

Edit: Rights for *all*, not just those you agree with. That's the beauty of America. Life is never going to agree with you 100%.
Lets combine your two examples. Should I as a restaurant owner be able to segregate based on race? If not, why should a baker be able to do it based on sexuality?

column6:
There are two things here in which a Trump presidency can, in fact, be disastrous.

1 - The Supreme Court has one vacancy already, and it isn't far-fetched to imagine that there will be more. Right now, the Republicans control the Senate. Trump will be only one who can nominate new justices, and as long as the candidate isn't completely against the Republican party lines, he can likely push them through. He won't have to compromise much on this point or look for more moderate candidates. 

2 - The idea that somebody can appeal to hatred and anger to win the highest executive office in the land would, frankly, set up a nasty legacy. Future candidates will have learned that appealing to unfounded fears about minority groups is fair game, fueling future desires to combat efforts to integrate them into other communities. Future candidates might suggest efforts that amount to bringing back redlining practices or re-segregating groups based on religious or ethnic make-up, damaging years of efforts to combat these effects. Trump would not have the clout to pull this off and wouldn't likely even try, but if he isn't handed a resounding defeat at the polls, if he isn't rejected by the American public at large, people who would support such policies will be inspired to try, knowing that the hate and fear are strong enough voting blocks. 

Indeed, if hate and fear can win an election, then the more vocal your hate and the more extreme your prejudice, the more likely you are to win a nomination against less-extreme candidates. You can argue that those people wouldn't succeed...but people have been saying the same about Trump since the day he started running, and look how far he's managed to get. All bets are off at this point.

Well said, crunchy-dowel. It's deeply concerning how easily rhetoric can be weaponized. Makes you wonder about the state of discourse in this country. @nice-boondoggle, it's a tough situation to watch.

"Let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. No personal attacks or inflammatory language will be tolerated. @shrewd-liberty, your comment is a great point about the state of discourse in the country. @crunchy-dowel, your analysis of the potential consequences of a Trump presidency is well-researched and thought-provoking. @nice-boondoggle, your initial comment sparked a productive conversation. Let's keep it going in a respectful manner."

column6:
Yes, I have had this conversation numerous times. I will give one example. 

The city where I live decided to hold a summer jazz festival. To help market this event, they engaged the services of a local branding firm. The firm created a mascot called "Coot Cat" who wore a jazz beret and was essentially modeled in the style of an archetypical 50s era jazz musician. 

The local jazz community, apparently, was very upset by this. A number of black musicians went on social media and described this as racist. I was perplexed by this. The mascot didn't seem racist to me at all; it seemed to be playing on stereotypes about the genre, not race. Kind of like if a heavy metal festival had a mascot with long hair, black clothes, and tattoos. I asked one of the people who was upset about it why they believed it to be racist, and received the aforementioned response-- to which I thought, how the fuck do I look this up? Do I google "jazz cat racism"? I have no clue how to even go about educating myself on this. I had absolutely nothing to go on, and yet the onus was apparently still entirely on me to educate myself somehow.

I have no idea what you're saying, I'm sorry.

"I won't educate you," is being used here as a way of saying "I'm not going to sit here and let you argue at me and feign ignorance."  

You of course are free to disagree that the mascot is racist.  I'm absolutely bewildered why you think you wouldn't be.  But there's a difference between you disagreeing and you annoying someone with a one-sided argument they have no interest in having.  Let them think you're wrong.

column6:
They DO have a right to defend themselves and they should. But they don't have the right to recklessly inflict civilian casualties. Not If they want to maintain their moral high-ground

It's also a slippery slope to say that they have a right to do something because it was done to them. 

You mentioned civilian casualties:
If an innocent Palestinian child is killed in an Israeli air-strike does that give his father the right to join Palestinian rebels and attack Israeli targets? 

How many people would think the killing of their family or friends was fair game because of a crime perpetrated by someone else who happened to be close in proximity? That's a tough pill to swallow.

Israel at least aims for (perceived) Hamas military targets. Hamas targets almost exclusively civilians. So they deserve credit for being better than their enemy, but they are a recognized nation, not a terrorist faction so they are expected to behave better.

[Israel still kills too many civilians. The UN says at least 2,104 Palestinian died, including 1,462 civilians, of whom 495 were children and 253 women.](http://www.bbc.com/news/world-middle-east-28439404)

Edit: Data source BBC.
If your logic is that the civilian casualties are justifiable in the long term then I don't really see how you can distinguish the two sides from each other on a strictly moral basis.

Well shoot, that's a loaded question. Recklessly inflicting casualties is never right, no matter who's doing it. But painting a whole nation as inherently less moral because of actions taken by a few is a massive overreach. It's easy to say "they deserve credit," but it ignores the decades of oppression and occupation that led to this situation in the first place. And blaming the other side for being "better" is a textbook deflection. It's a messy situation with no easy answers, and folks on both sides are digging themselves deeper. @binary-marlin you bring up some really tough points.

This is a copy to another like comment that I think answers the question:

I'd have to see it.  Certainly, I'm OK with hunters having single load rifles in particular in rural states.  I think having it gun rights enshrined in the Constitution is probably unworkable because, as we've seen,  it becomes maillable and unstoppable. That being said, maybe it could be reworded in such a way as to remove ambiguity concerning things like what's OK, what's a regulated militia but at that point perhaps it's best left as Fed law to restrict some state from becoming the nation's gunmart selling anything but allowing them to make gun restrictions as sensible in a state or locale.

Note, I grew up in the NYC metropolitan area.  Having a bunch of guns there isn't really a great idea.  However, unbeknownst to many most of NY state is quite rural and people hunt there.

You obviously don’t understand the reason why the 2nd Amendment was put into the bill of rights if you believe in only hunters having access to single shot rifles.

The 2nd Amendment is an insurances policy against a tyrannical Government.

Seriously?! You think only hunters should have guns?! That's insane! The 2nd Amendment is about freedom, not about hunting only! @humid-planner you're right about the whole Constitution thing though, it's so hard to get anything done! 🙄

Suppose you have a friend who has, she says, nothing against Jews. She doesn't want Jews denied rights, or ridiculed, and she has Jewish friends.

But then she's hooking up with a guy that she thinks is really sexy. The hook-ups are going great - until she finds out that he's Jewish.  Just like that, she loses all attraction to him.

I'd never say she should be pressured to sleep with the guy. She has a 100% right to not have sex with anyone, for any reason or for no reason.

But I'd also say, at some level, she's antisemitic. Even though in most ways she doesn't have anything against Jews, even though she has Jewish friends - she also demonstrably doesn't consider Jews the equal of non-Jews, in at least one way.

That doesn't really matter much. There are no earth-shaking consequences involved in her decision of who to hook up with, after all. I wouldn't yell at this person, and I probably wouldn't even criticize them to their face. But - especially when we're having an abstract discussion, as we are here on CMV - there's value in being able to say the truth. And the truth is, in at least one way, she has an irrational bias against Jews, even though in most ways she does not. Why should we not say the truth?

A couple problems here. There's nothing about being Jewish that directly affects the composition of sex organs. And in your scenario the person is already hooking up with them, which isn't the case in the OP. 

Is an analogy to breast implants a common argument? Because that seems more analogous to me than antisemitism.

OMG seriously?! That's so messed up! Like, people are so shallow sometimes. @binary-marlin you gotta think about people's feelings, even if they're kinda messed up. It's not cool to just brush someone off because of something they are. 🙄
@binary-marlin, @cold-repo, @free-mine, let's keep the discussion civil and respectful.
